data.title: Coupang's Infrastructure Advantage Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Coupang's (CPNG) investment case is based upon the significant logistical structure that it has developed, making it a critical part of the national delivery infrastructure rather than just a traditional e-commerce platform. The company has built over 100 logistics/delivery centers throughout South Korea, with 70% of citizens living within 7 miles of a Coupang distribution center. With this large-scale logistical structure, the company has established an infrastructure moat around their logistics operations. The combination of Coupang's logistics infrastructure and fast delivery times creates an opportunity to expand margins as automation technology becomes increasingly adopted in its operations. Given that automation technology currently accounts for ~10% of Coupang's total infrastructure, there are opportunities for cost savings that could help improve sentiment without continued revenue growth.

Coupang has begun a transition from investing heavily in new businesses and technologies to focusing on cash flow. This is particularly apparent in the Product Commerce division, which produces solid margins of 8.4% and strong EBITDA growth. However, consolidated profitability has remained depressed as a result of investments made in Taiwan, Eats, and the integration of Farfetch. These three areas represent potential future sources of earnings in the long-term story, with early indicators suggesting that both the Taiwan and Farfetch divisions could grow into meaningful sources of profitability. The company has also established a WOW membership program, designed to retain customers through loyalty programs and increase customer spending. This contributes to higher spend and increased monetization, creating a long-term flywheel that increases customer value. With short-term noise overshadowing the company's long-term potential, I'm rating the stock a Strong Buy.

A Logistics Fortress

Coupang's primary competitive advantage is its complex logistical network. The company now operates over 100 fulfillment centers throughout South Korea, owns its own private fleet for last-mile delivery, and has invested billions to build out this physical network. The result is that nearly 70% of South Korea is within 7 miles of a Coupang distribution center, allowing next-day delivery.

The difficulty in replicating this network is both due to the level of investment required as well as the years of operational experience already embedded at the company. In 2024 alone, Coupang was able to increase same-day and dawn deliveries by 45% while reducing line haul costs by 16% using a combination of robotic technology and process improvements. Despite this improvement, the automated share of total infrastructure is currently in the very low teens. Management has continually highlighted the potential for additional margin gains through continued use of automated processes, which could set them further apart from competitors. With the company's market share of ~40%, Coupang has opportunities to grow and become more efficient, making today's levels appear undervalued.

High-Burn to Cash Generation

Adjusted EBITDA for Product Commerce rose to $2.485 billion in FY 2025, up from $2.0 billion in FY 2024. Segment margins improved to 8.4% YoY, showing signs of operational leverage. Growth rates for revenue increased 16% in constant currency, and gross profit grew 22% in constant currency, reflecting the increasing impact from the FLC third-party marketplace offering, where revenues are reported on a net basis. This compresses revenue growth while gross profit continues to outpace it, signaling that the business mix may be improving.

Overall, revenues for the company totaled $34.5 billion for FY 2025, up 14% YoY and 18% in constant currency, with gross margin increasing to 29.4%. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for Coupang was $1.49 billion at a margin of 4.3%; however, this number is impacted negatively by losses (~$995 million) from Taiwan development, Farfetch integration, and Eats expansion. This makes the Product Commerce margin of 8.4% a better indicator of earnings power.

FCF was $527 million in FY 2025 versus $1 billion in FY 2024 due to the data incident, which increased working capital, as well as greater investments in automation. The company also has a solid balance sheet with ~$6.3 billion in cash vs. ~$4.6 billion in debt, resulting in a net cash position of $1.68 billion. Due to this, management has been returning capital by purchasing back $243 million worth of its stock in 2025. This signals a shift away from heavy reinvestment and towards increasing shareholder returns, with a total authorization of $1 billion for buybacks. Coupang's improved balance sheet (with $6 billion+ in cash) also supports this and allows for flexibility to either invest in growth or continue to return value back to shareholders. Although there has been some hiccup in results, the long-term trajectory remains the same, giving investors the chance to buy a maturing high-growth business at a discount.

Beyond Korea

Coupang’s launch of Rocket Delivery into Taiwan during late 2022 has shown strong results. Despite heavy near-term investment in this region, management has highlighted triple-digit revenue growth in Taiwan, suggesting that the long-term payoff will be worth it as the market matures. By the end of 2024, revenue in Taiwan grew by 23% QoQ, and this momentum carried into 2025. Besides Rocket Delivery, Coupang has introduced their WOW membership program into Taiwan, showing signs of sufficient market density and consumer trust.

When Coupang purchased Farfetch in early 2024, Farfetch was losing hundreds of millions annually with declining growth metrics. One year after acquiring the company, Farfetch improved from heavy losses to a breakeven run rate while maintaining 49 million monthly site visitors and ~$4 billion in annual transaction volume globally. Management’s outlook involves further streamlining operations and removing what it described as “non-reflective” cost structures, which should help improve gross margins as integration matures.

With this acquisition, the company has positioned itself as a legitimate player in the global luxury goods market, opening up more growth potential beyond its flagship e-commerce business.

Net revenue for Developing Offerings was $4.94 billion during FY 2025, a 38% increase YoY and a 40% YoY increase in constant currency. The losses of $995 million were due to investments in infrastructure in Taiwan and costs associated with integrating Farfetch and were not structural issues with the segment. These developments give Coupang optionality and could contribute meaningfully as they continue to scale and improve, which is potential that doesn't currently appear to be factored into the valuation.

The WOW Flywheel

Coupang’s WOW membership program is probably one of the most successful retention programs in Asian e-commerce. During mid-2024, the company raised the monthly fee by 58%, which many assumed would lead to massive churn. It did not. Before that, in 2021, the company increased the price of the membership by 72%, which also had very few member losses. The reason members continue to stay is because active WOW members who use all five benefits will save ~970,000 won per year (~$655), which is much more than the annual membership cost of ~95,000 won. This makes the value proposition attractive and allows them to maintain members despite price increases.

WOW membership experienced cancellations due to the data incident in December 2025, something management admitted on the Q4 2025 earnings call. However, a recovery trend in WOW memberships began to appear once it became clear no sensitive information was compromised. With this recovery and the stock still trading below prior levels, it creates a buying opportunity. What makes WOW so important to Coupang is the effects it has on the rest of the business. Members shop more frequently, spend more per visit, and have much higher lifetime values. This creates an amplifier effect on advertising and fintech products from Coupang, where a denser active member base creates greater demand from third-party sellers/advertisers. This creates a compounding loop that makes the Korean business hard to replace.

Upcoming Earnings

There are three key factors to watch as Coupang reports its Q1 earnings. These include the company's ability to leverage automation to increase margins, narrow losses in “Developing Offerings,” and continue WOW member recovery. Product Commerce's adjusted EBITDA margin is a key metric, and the continued growth of this metric (from 8.4% in FY 2025) will signal improvement in automation and strengthen its logistics moat. Management still has confidence in the long-term future for the Product Commerce segment and believes that it will be able to achieve 10%+ EBITDA margins. This expansion is expected to be driven by operational efficiencies, supply chain optimization, and operating leverage, alongside a shift toward higher-margin product mix. Another aspect to watch is improvement in the profitability of the Taiwan and Farfetch businesses, where the market may be expecting improvement as operations mature.

Finally, it will be important to hear commentary on WOW membership trends post-data incident. Management has already noted that the impact from the data incident began to stabilize across growth rates, active customers, and WOW memberships in early Q1 2026, suggesting underlying demand remains intact. If Coupang can reaffirm member retention rates have returned to pre-data incident levels, it will provide further confirmation of the resiliency of Coupang’s flywheel model. This will suggest that the company has sufficient pricing power and market share to continue to operate profitably, despite competition from lower-cost players. Strong or weak metrics in these areas could have a huge impact on investor sentiment.

Valuation

At the current price of ~$21, Coupang is trading at around 1.04x EV/TTM sales and ~35x EV/TTM EBITDA. It should be noted that the EV/EBITDA number is somewhat depressed by the ~$995M in Developing Offerings investment losses. As such, using the Product Commerce segment as a base would place the company at ~14x EV/EBITDA (with $2.49 billion in segment-adjusted EBITDA). This represents a relatively low multiple for the company's strong competitive advantage combined with 88 bps of annual margin expansion and expanding automation efficiency.

For FY2026, I expect adjusted EBITDA to reach ~$2.8 Billion due to the resumption of growth through mid-single to high single-digit rates on a constant-currency basis as well as continuing margin expansion. I also project that Developing Offerings will reduce their net loss to the ~$600-$700 Million range as Farfetch reaches profitability and Taiwan grows. This brings total adjusted EBITDA for the company to ~$2.1-2.2 billion for FY2026. Applying a 22x EV/EBITDA, which is similar to peers such as Sea Ltd.'s (SE) and Amazon (AMZN), brings EV to $48 billion. Adding $1.68 billion in net cash and dividing by 1.83 billion shares outstanding gives a stock price of ~$27. I believe this is a fair 12-month price target given Taiwan optionality and the potential for Farfetch to recover.

Risks to the Thesis

The major risk to Coupang’s stock is regulation, with investigations still ongoing. This makes potential fines or regulatory actions uncertain. Forensic analysis was completed by Mandiant and Palo Alto Networks, which included both digital forensics and threat hunting activities, and neither could find evidence of customers' personal financial information being compromised. Coupang also compensated the 33.7 million customers affected with vouchers worth 50,000 won, which could help keep users on the platform. This suggests the incident is a near-term headwind rather than a long-term drag.

AliExpress and Temu are gaining market share in Korean e-commerce markets through low pricing, creating competition for Coupang. However, the fact that the company has continued to raise its WOW membership fee demonstrates that the price alone does not necessarily drive away existing customers. Coupang also offers a number of additional services, including dawn delivery, fresh produce, free returns, OTT streaming, and integrated financial services, which cannot currently be replicated by any cross-border marketplace.

Takeaway

Coupang represents a strong combination of an infrastructure moat, improving operating margins, and undervalued international operations. The market appears to have discounted both the ability of the Korean segment to generate profits in the short term, as well as the potential profitability of newer segments in the long term. This creates a disconnect from the stock's fair value, which was caused by short-term headwinds.

As the business continues to automate at scale, normalize investments, and grow new geographic markets, it has the opportunity to expand profitability. With a big change in the stock's valuation but little change in the long-term story, I'm rating the stock a Strong Buy with a 12-month price target of ~$27.

data.title: Western Digital: This Bull Run May Just Be Starting
data.description:
Western Digital Corporation (WDC) creates and sells data storage devices and solutions based on hard disk drive technology. The firm is set to report FY 26 Q3 earnings with expectations of massive YoY expansion on earnings and sales. The stock is up nearly 9x in the last year alone, and investors that have not gotten in on the gains yet are left wondering if it's too late.

Their products are used in everything from laptops to large cloud data centers. Almost 90% of their revenue now comes from sales in the cloud space to specific cloud providers and enterprise data centers. A tight supply and rising demand environment have led to capped-out capacity for WDC, a good and bad thing. The industry is seeing explosive growth, and longer-term contracts are giving suppliers like WDC more pricing power than in the past. Valuation is raised, but not horribly. Because of these industry headwinds and their ability to create greater margins on more efficient exabyte shipments, I have WDC as a buy.

Q3 Expectations

Adjusted EPS is projected to arrive at $2.39 per share, which would be a YoY change of 75% greater. All 20 analysts on Seeking Alpha have given the stock upward revisions in the last 90 days. You can see the growth above in EPS estimates and how rapid it is expected, especially in the next 2-3 years.

Another piece that I am bullish about is where I think they sit in the hard disk drive cycle. Amidst the growth the stock has seen by price return, earnings, and demand, revenue has actually been lower YoY for 4 straight quarters. This week they are anticipated to report sales of $3.23 billion, which would reverse the recent trend and put them 40% higher YoY. I think this can be a huge sign that even though the stock has seen huge growth, this could actually be in the early stages of a new cycle. Storage prices are cyclical, and WDC saw a big drop in revenue over the last few years. This could actually be the beginning of a new upcycle for them, exactly what investors should want to hear.

A few things to look out for in management commentary and reported data this week are what they are seeing on prices per exabyte, how many exabytes are being shipped, any new long-term commitments, and what their plan is to continue increasing efficiency at an already full capacity. They have outlined that they expect to be north of 50% in gross margin and 40% in operating margin, which was actually negative not too long ago in 2024. Also, I will be curious to see further comments on the AI demand from the large data center customers. WDC has seen their revenue from their top 10 customers grow from 65% to 77% in the first 6 months of the fiscal year over the last two years. This comes as these big-time AI infrastructure-needy customers continue to up their needs. This is good, but with a cyclical industry, you don't want too high of a dependence on just a few customers.

Growing Exabytes Are the Answer

When WDC “ships” more exabytes, it means that they sold more drives or higher capacity that can store more data, which leads to greater sales. In Q2 they increased their exabytes shipped by over 20% YoY and are now on 4 straight quarters of growth here. This is the real indicator of demand for their product, which is primarily driven by large hyperscalers, like AWS and Microsoft, for example. Their cloud segment has come to dominate their revenue totals as these cloud-storage providers look for hard disk drives for massive datasets. Long-term agreements are becoming the norm in this space, and WDC has publicized that they have established two long-term agreements for 2027 and already one for 2028. They note that they are set with purchase orders from each of their seven biggest customers.

A massive driver for WDC is the price per terabyte (TB) for hard drives. If you look at the firm's history of revenue, it's not hard to realize that price per TB peaked in the mid-2010s, as did WDC revenue. In a rough period from 2018 to 2022, tech improvements increased storage density, and cloud customers gained pricing power. These customers also overbought and laid off purchases through a lot of the last several years. Price per TB was north of $30 a decade ago, has dropped down near $13 in 2024, and now looks to be on the horizon of a rebound as demand rebounds strongly.

The fact that WDC still has relatively low revenue based on historical numbers and has been able to grow earnings like they have, I think, is a great sign. Especially as it seems they are on the cusp of reporting their first YoY revenue win in a while, marking a sign that the tides could be turning.

Efficient Growth

WDC has said that their capacity for 2026 is already fully booked. This is a fantastic sign for demand purposes but leaves investors wondering how they could grow from here. They, of course, can slowly build on more operations, but management has made it clear that is not the key to the growth plan.

They are rapidly advancing higher-capacity drives, which are typically around 20TB today. They have 40TB drives that are already in customer testing, with a roadmap to scale to 60TB and even 100TB over time, dramatically increasing total exabytes shipped per unit. They plan for deployment of the 40TB drives later this year and for 100TB drives by 2029. So, instead of needing to sell more units, WDC is well into the process of increasing exabytes shipped by improving their hard drive storage capacity.

Risk to Thesis & Peers

WDC has seen their PE ratio rise from 8x to 38x in the last year. That's obviously a risk and a large rise in valuation. Compared to the industry, they are actually only slightly above the sector median, which is giving me peace of mind from this perspective. Price to sales is 4x the sector median, but their PEG is astronomically low at 0.04x, which shows clear expectations of growth that I have already outlined their path toward. I think that the stock is slightly overvalued, but I am not concerned. The elevated multiples will compress quickly if their earnings rise as expected this year, by estimates of around 82%.

Competitor Seagate Tech (STX) is another key player in this field that has also seen a massive price return of 6.5x in the last year. They are also working toward increasing TB per drive and have already rolled out drives of 30TB+. I think there is more than enough demand in the cloud-driven world that WDC does not need to worry about losing out. But it is a competitive space, and there are others honing in on similar strategies. Comparing further, WDC has had slightly better growth in sales than STX and is valued at much more of a discount, as STX currently carries a PE of 67x. STX at the moment is more of the pure-play hard disk drive investment, whereas WDC has a more diversified structure. However, the spinoff of Sandisk (SNDK) from WDC has led the company to focus more on the hard disk drive-specific space, while the latter takes on more of the flash (NAND) chip space.

Conclusion

I'm expecting big things from the earnings report this week for WDC. There has been huge growth in earnings and margins in recent quarters as the firm picks up more long-term contracts from cloud providers. Revenue has been lagging in recent years as the price per TB for hard drives is near the bottom of its cycle. I anticipate a huge revenue rebound and a spark to what could be just the beginning of an already strong run. I predict exabytes shipped to have another strong showing and be near 230 for Q3. Look for management commentary on the rollout of their 40TB drive, as this could be the most efficient in the game over other peers if it is finalized this year.

Even though WDC has seen tremendous price return, I think we may look back at this period as an opportunity to buy this stock at a discount. My opinion is that we are entering a multiple-year structural upcycle that will come with big earnings and huge profit boosts. I think many things have aligned for a strong run for WDC. Value is only slightly inflated, earnings and demand are booming, and revenue is about to get the spark it needs as hard disk drive pricing begins its climb again. Buy WDC.

data.title: Net Lease Office Properties: A $21 Bear Case For A $13 Stock
data.description:
Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) is a special situation REIT in the middle of a portfolio liquidation. NLOP was spun off from W. P. Carey (WPC) in 2023 and has been steadily selling assets ever since, shrinking its portfolio from 59 to 18. This is the capital being returned to shareholders through the ~50%+ forward yield.

In order to understand if NLOP is still a buy, we need to ask, what are the remaining 18 properties worth?

Based on the current annualized base rent of ~$26M and reasonable market cap rates, these 18 properties appear to support a liquidation value in the $20-22/sh range, well above the current ~$13 price. This gap between the potential asset sale value and the market’s frankly pessimistic outlook creates a cautious buying opportunity.

An Overview of NLOP

NLOP's stated business plan is "realizing value for our shareholders primarily through strategic asset management and disposition of our property portfolio."

At its origin, it had 59 single-tenant office properties generating around $145M in annualized rent. Currently, only 18 properties are left, which yield ~$26M in rent—implying that the higher-value properties were skimmed off in the earlier transactions. Point to remember for valuation later: these 18 should not fetch the same cap rates as the other 41.

Anyway, proceeds from those sales were used to repay $455M of debt, and shareholders were paid multiple large special dividends. This gives us our framing—NLOP is a shrinking asset base being converted into cash, and traditional REIT metrics do not apply here. The only thing that matters is what price they can net for these properties and whether that is more than their current equity value.

The Remaining 18 Properties

These 18 properties have 79% occupancy (Dec 2025 figure) with a ~3.0-year weighted average lease term. That means this is not a table portfolio, partially vacant, shorter-duration, relatively speaking, and heavily dependent on individual tenants—not a chain. This directly impacts valuation.

A few characteristics define the remaining portfolio. First, tenants are somewhat concentrated, so a large portion of rent comes from a handful of them. A single lease event, like a renewal or a termination, can materially impact realized value.

Second, with WALT of only ~3.0 years, there are near-term leasing risks for many properties. This sort of uncertainty will be priced into acquisition cap rates by buyers.

Third, the vacancy is concentrated rather than broad-based. The remaining portfolio table shows two vacant former BCBSM assets in Eagan, MN, totaling 454,828 square feet, or about 24% of the remaining 1.875M square feet. That concentration helps explain the occupancy figure, but these vacant assets are still a real risk because their sale value may be harder to underwrite.

The key challenge is office space headwinds. These are primarily single-tenant office properties, and comprising nearly 40% of all distressed CRE assets, it is arguably the most challenging segment in commercial real estate today.

While the above discussion directionally explains some of the steep discount, it doesn’t justify that discount altogether. While these are non-core locations, occupancy is still solid—and so shouldn’t be considered for extreme distress pricing. To analyse that, we will convert the rental figure to a proper liquidation value using realistic cap rates and, critically, actual transaction evidence from prior asset sales.

Valuing the 18 Properties

Our investment case rests on converting the remaining $26M ABR into liquidation value. There are no recurring earnings to project, no growth to model. Those 18 properties represent the entire outcome.

The first step is to get a proper cap rate. Office real estate today can have multiple cap rates; high-quality, long-lease assets could be 7-9%, average office could be 9-11%, and short-lease, secondary, vacancy-risk assets could be 10-13%, depending on location and other factors.

Given NLOP’s remaining portfolio has 75-79% occupancy and 3-year WALT, with meaningful tenant concentration but multiple smaller/secondary assets. This is not prime pricing, but I would argue for a 10-12% cap rate, not over that. The bull case would go lower.

Now look at the valuation scenarios using a $26.3M ABR:

Add balance sheet—cash of ~120M and debt of ~$22M:

Using shares outstanding: ~14.8M, we get NAV per share:

Now we can test these assumptions against actual transactions. NLOP has already sold a majority of its portfolio, providing real pricing data.

The results are mixed. Several assets cleared the 8-11% range. That KBR transaction is a highly distressed outlier. However, the market seems to be effectively pricing most of the remaining assets at distressed cap rates, near the KBR figure. In fact, if you simply take the implied EV of $101M and the $26M ABR rate, you get a ~26% cap rate. This is not supported by the realized transactions so far, nor by the details of the 18 remaining properties.

So I will take a 10-12% cap rate here, and that implies a liquidation value meaningfully above the current share price.

At ~$13/share (~$195M market cap), removing cash and adding debt, we get implied remaining properties worth $101M, the enterprise value of the company. Even our bear case is more than that. There are only two ways to reconcile this gap. Either we say that the market is correct to expect significant deterioration before those assets are sold, or it is over-discounting execution risk.

This gap between the modeled value and the market price is where the opportunity (or trap if you are looking at it from another angle) lies. The remaining question is not what the assets are truly worth, but whether they can be sold at those levels. As anyone who has dabbled in real estate knows—like this author, for example—what a property is worth and what you can get for it are not always the same number.

Risks to a Buy Thesis

Several risks exist and may account for why the market is valuing NLOP as a distressed REIT asset seller.

The first is a simple execution risk. We are assuming that management will be able to sell the remaining assets at cap rates much below distressed rates. However, real estate sales are negotiated deals, and there’s no formula that can tell you where one might end up. If buyer demand weakens or management loses patience, realized price could fall short of our modeled values.

The second one is the short lease profile and two vacant assets. Those two, probably, are not going to fetch high premiums. The short lease profile will also be a hurdle. If more tenants depart before sales can be closed, that would reduce both income and valuation.

A third risk is that the bulk of the higher-quality assets have largely been sold. Their cap rates and sale values may not be appropriate metrics for the rest. Indeed, that could perhaps be the precise reason these are still not sold. Plus, the original portfolio generated ~$145M of rent across 59 properties. The remaining 18 properties generate only ~$26M, which means these are not as prime as the other 41.

A fourth risk is the tenant concentration. The top tenant alone accounts for $4.1M or ~15% of ABR, while the top 5 account for ~$16M+ (~60%+ of ABR). That means if these tenants leave, property value does not hold.

The overarching risk is, of course, the market condition of commercial real estate, where office real estate remains the most distressed. Taken together, these may justify part of the market reaction—especially as opposed to our bull case.

Conclusion

Given the above risk profile, I am not able to fully justify the market reaction vis-a-vis my bear case, which still gives me a share price of $21, nearly 60% above the current market price. While the market is surely pricing in all the risks I discussed, and possibly more besides, it does appear that the gap is too much. The company has managed to close many transactions within a closed cluster of 7-11% cap rate. There are 4-5 outliers that are obvious distress sales, but these are just that—outliers. Unless we consider all these 18 properties as outliers, a 10-12% cap rate is justifiable.

That is why I rate NLOP a cautious Buy at current levels.

data.title: UMB Financial: Momentum Accelerates In A Strong Q1
data.description:
Shares of UMB Financial (UMBF) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 30% as the company’s acquisition of Heartland is showing increased financial benefits. In Q1, we saw an acceleration of these trends. Importantly, UMBF’s conservative underwriting has also left it with strong credit quality and very well protected from pressures in private credit. I last covered shares in December, upgrading UMB to a “Buy,” and that call has played out with shares up 10% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit UMBF.

In the company’s first quarter, UMB Financial earned $3.41 per share in adjusted EPS, up from $2.58 last year. This blew past consensus by $0.59 thanks to faster loan growth and strong cost discipline. UMB is now realizing the cost synergies from its Heartland deal as well as benefits from its low-cost deposit base. Beyond this, ongoing balance sheet growth and the rollover of low-yielding securities are further boosting earnings. These organic and inorganic actions have positioned UMB for significant ongoing earnings growth over the course of 2026, in my view.

Looking first at its balance sheet, average deposits were flat sequentially at $57.6 billion. Within this, we saw a favorable mix shift with non-interest-bearing balances (“NIB”) rising by $400 million thanks to ongoing growth in its trust department. These balances are now $15.1 billion, and after sustained pressure, NIBs have returned to a growth trajectory, which is very beneficial to margins. Interest-bearing deposit costs were down an impressive 24bps to 2.79%, reflecting a strong pass-through of rate cuts. As higher-cost CDs mature, I expect to see some further improvement this year.

During the quarter, average loans grew 11% annualized from Q4 to $39.4 billion, led by ongoing strength in C&I lending. Heartland’s branches were less productive than UMBF’s, and the company is now using that dry powder to close the gap and accelerate lending. Adjusted loan yields were down 15bps from Q4, reflecting the pull-through of Q4 rate cuts. Its loan portfolio is fairly diverse. It has $10.8 billion of commercial real estate and construction exposure, under 30% of its portfolio, a healthy level.

Now, an area of increased focus on bank earnings this quarter is non-depository financial institution (“NDFI”) lending, as this is the channel through which banks typically have private credit exposure. UMBF is a small player in this space with $2.6 billion of exposure, or just 6.6% of loans—many regional banks are above 10%. Historically, this loan book has exhibited very strong credit performance with just $225,000 of charge-offs over the past twenty years.

That is because not all NDFI exposure is equally risky. One of UMBF’s largest exposures is to subscription lines, which is generally very low risk. When a private equity fund raises capital, its end investors (major institutions) make a legal commitment but do not provide the cash until an investment is made. These lines allow the private equity fund to close on acquisitions while it “calls” capital from investors. UMBF is not actually exposed to the underlying business the fund is buying; its real exposure is to the sovereign wealth fund or pension fund that owes the cash. These are generally short-duration and very low-risk loans, not the type of “private credit” issues that have spooked markets.

UMBF’s most private credit-centric exposure is business credit intermediation. Here, it provides debt backed by underlying loans, but there is a 50+% haircut to the underlying loans, so there would need to be extraordinary losses before UMBF faces a loss. Even then, this entire exposure is just 0.5% of loans. I view UMBF as an extremely safe name amid private credit fears.

UMBF has a $20 billion securities portfolio, which sits on a $907 million loss because bonds were bought when interest rates were lower. As these bonds mature, UMBF can reinvest proceeds at market yields, helping to drive incremental interest income. Over the next year, it has $2.2 billion maturing at 3.66%, which should provide for an annualized $11-$15 million pre-tax income opportunity. I also expect UMBF to let this portfolio get gradually smaller and reinvest proceeds to support loan growth, which would further enhance margins.

When one bank acquires another, there can be significant accounting noise as assets are marked to fair value and then “accrete” back to par, which can distort net interest margin (“NIM”). Net accretion was a $51 million tailwind in Q1, and about $560 million remains. In noninterest categories, there are $403 million of remaining expenses. While reported NIM was 3.38%, its adjusted NIM for these factors was 3.05%, which was up 9bps sequentially and from 2.75% a year ago, as the company benefits from fixed reinvestment. I expect further improvement this year.

Turning to credit quality, nonperforming loans (“NPLs”) ticked up 1bp to 0.38%. However, delinquencies actually fell to $55 million, the lowest in over a year, partly reflecting the typical seasonal improvement in Q1. Net charge-offs were 19bps, a healthy level, though up from 13bps in Q4.

During the quarter, UMB set aside $27 million for credit losses. Allowances now cover 1.06% of loans. This represents 2.8x of NPLs, above the 2.5x level that I view as very strong. UMB’s current reserve posture is fairly conservative, meaning that it should not have to meaningfully add to reserves even if we see credit trends worsen somewhat. I view UMBF as being very conservatively positioned with its reserves.

UMBF maintains a conservative capital position with an 11.2% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio. Given its size, it does not need to include the unrealized loss in AOCI in its capital calculation. Even if UMBF did, its CET1 would be 10.5%. I believe UMBF needs 10%-10.5% to safely run its business, so its capital position is solid. As a result, capital returns are secure and set to accelerate.

Shares offer a 1.4% dividend yield. Alongside results, the company also announced a new 2 million share buyback authorization for the next year. Entering 2026, I did not expect any material buyback activity, as it focused on organic growth, but it appears we may see some repurchases. This authorization could reduce the share count by about 2.5%, though I do not expect it to be fully utilized in 2026.

Back in December, I was targeting $11.25-$11.60 in 2026 EPS. Frankly, we are seeing a quicker realization of Heartland synergies than I expected, and I see UMBF earning $12.50 this year. With buybacks starting, a clean loan portfolio, and a solid capital position, I believe shares should trade towards the upper end of the 9-12x range most regional banks trade at. That points to shares moving past $140 this year. As a result, UMBF remains a “Buy” and is one of my preferred names in the regional banking sector.

data.title: NuScale: Why The ENTRA1 Cash Drain Justifies A Hold
data.description:
This is my first coverage of NuScale Power (SMR). Recently, I wrote an article on Oklo, and I would like to further explore the nuclear sector. If you are interested in the Oklo article, you can read it here. Despite the company having NRC approval of their small modular reactors, I am rating the company as a "Hold". This is based on the long timeline to wider technology adoptions and large potential expenses due to milestone payments. I will also analyze their cash position and provide my opinion on what to look for in the upcoming Q1 earnings release.

Introduction and Business Model

Unlike Oklo, NuScale utilizes an asset-light business model. They are offering engineering services and selling their technology—SMRs. Besides that, they also offer front-end engineering and design services (FEED), to utility companies.

To be specific, they rely on their global partner, ENTRA1 Energy, which actually deals with the customers and operates the reactors once they are built. ENTRA1 relies on subcontractors (like Fluor) that provide construction, engineering, and other miscellaneous services. I believe this leaves NuScale in a disadvantageous position, as they are at the mercy of the main partner's ability to secure financing and to agree on the nuclear contracts. Additionally, this is the complete opposite of vertical integration, which makes it likely that only a small part of the total revenues would go to NuScale while being completely dependent on the partners. Still, I could be wrong, so let’s dig deeper into ongoing projects.

The Romanian RoPower Project

The pivotal project that will serve as a confirmation for NuScale’s technology’s commercial viability is the project in Romania. The aim is to deploy six 77 MW modules at a former coal power plant. The customer will first pay for only one 77 MW module, with the remaining five to be paid for only after it is proven that the first one is functioning properly. I believe that it might take a few years until that first module is completed, making NuScale burn cash for a while. Some evidence can be found in the earnings call. Management stated, "RoPower is now authorized to advance the licensing in geotechnical moving towards a pre-EPC, and we anticipate NuScale will be generating revenues as soon as -- now understand, we are a subcontractor to Fluor Corporation," and "we anticipate that they will have pre-EPC activities begin in the second quarter of this year and have an estimated duration of up to 15 months."

Based on this, it should take approx. a year for pre-EPC to be completed, and I estimate building of the physical reactor will take even longer. Management did not give an exact timeline, but I am skeptical it could be finished before 2030. As such, the company will probably have to raise cash, and if there is no stock price trend reversal, they will have to do it at these depressed prices. This should serve as a negative catalyst for the stock.

Additionally, in that same quote, the management stated that they are a subcontractor to Fluor Corporation. I dislike that they do not have direct control, but instead they only serve as a subcontractor and not a company leading the project. Consequently, if there are any delays, it would be much more devastating for NuScale than for a large multinational company like Fluor. Since this is a new technology, it is quite likely there will be some delays, which leaves NuScale too exposed for my liking. I would be much more confident investing in NuScale once this project is closer to completion.

I would also like to point out that in the past, Utah Associated Municipal Power Systems terminated a similar project, which was expected to include six 77-MW modules. I am not saying the same would happen in Romania, but we shouldn’t discount this possibility, especially if there are significant delays.

Tennessee Valley Authority Agreement and Cash Drain

In September 2025, the Tennessee Valley Authority announced it had signed an agreement with ENTRA1 Energy to develop up to 6 GW of new nuclear power in the largest U.S. small modular reactor deployment program to date. Considering one module provides 77 MW of power, this seems like a really positive agreement. I agree if they manage to successfully build out the power plants, but in the short term, it could be quite problematic.

In the Q4 earnings call, the CFO noted, "We noted a payment of about $250 million out. Arithmetically, we can assume about $1 billion of cash on balance sheet today." Although we do not know exactly what this payment was for, I expect it was a partnership milestone payment to ENTRA1. Both companies signed an agreement that obligates specific payments to ENTRA1 based on certain milestones. You can find the details of the agreement here.

Since the contract was reached with the Tennessee Valley Authority, NuScale had to pay 15% of the milestone contribution, even if the agreement is non-binding. If this 15% of the contribution was actually those $250 million mentioned, the total milestone contribution if the contract is fully realized would amount to approx. $1.6 billion. This is an enormous amount, and I am highly skeptical how NuScale could make enough revenue from the contract to cover these milestone payments. I believe a prudent investor should watch from the sidelines to see how the contract will evolve.

Valuation and Liquidity

First, I have to point out that currently it is futile to try to evaluate NuScale on metrics like PE, PS, or EV/EBITDA multiples. The company makes minuscule revenues, and the current valuation is based on the future potential of the company.

Next, we need to analyze cash reserves to determine if they need to raise cash in the near future. Based on the quote in the previous chapter, management mentioned having about $1 billion in cash on the balance sheet. Management also noted that "If I look at my OpEx, apart from what I would call one-time-type items, my OpEx stays fairly consistent between $170 million and, call it, $200 million." Although these are operating expenses, I doubt CapEx would be high, since the company is currently only a technology provider, and the construction of the Romanian power facility will not commence in 2026. I believe it to be unlikely that the company will need to raise cash in 2026.

Q1 2026 Expectations

I would also like to provide what we should look for in the Q1 2026 earnings results. I will not be watching for any meaningful revenue change or any other valuation metrics. What I am the most interested in is management providing further clarity on both contracts: RoPower and Tennessee Valley Authority. Although I highly doubt there will be important milestones reached, any positive update could provide a basis for a trend reversal from the currently depressed prices. For me, any such positive news would serve as a "Sell the News" event, as I believe the road to profitability or to the wider adoption of the technology will be long.

What would be the most meaningful is some kind of binding agreement with the Tennessee Valley Authority. If such a development were to happen, I would most likely change my rating from a "Hold" to a "Buy"

Plan

I currently do not hold any stock, but even though my article may sound bearish, I would not consider shorting the stock. I believe that due to the technical analysis, with the stock being near 52-week lows and the short interest being above 20%, it is extremely risky to initiate a short position. Thus, I will stand on the sidelines, read the earnings transcript carefully, and I will provide an update in the comments if my thesis changes.

data.title: Allspring Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets (EM) Index (Net), over the three-month period that ended March 31, 2026. The fund also outperformed its benchmark over the 12-month period.
  • The largest contributors to quarterly performance included Korea, Poland, information technology (IT), and financials while the largest detractors included India, China, communication services, and consumer discretionary.

Market review

The MSCI EM Index (Net) declined 0.17% in the quarter as a correction in March overwhelmed strong gains in January and February.

Conflict in Iran and disruption to Middle East energy supplies, dollar strength, artificial intelligence (AI) investment, and macroeconomic headwinds in India and China were market drivers. In January, Korea regulatory reforms and memory chip demand were strong tailwinds, and Latin American countries largely brushed off the U.S. incursion into Venezuela. In February, companies in the AI supply chain continued to lead, China tech platforms fell on fears of disruption from AI agents and competition, Thailand elections signaled stability, and the Middle East braced for potential conflict. In March, the Iran conflict, higher oil prices, and inflation uncertainty weighed on markets while oil exporters Colombia and Saudi Arabia performed strongly.

Emerging market currencies fell 2.29% versus the U.S. dollar, as the Iran conflict encouraged central bankers to shelve interest rate cut plans and the dollar benefited from its perceived safe-haven status. Nineteen of 24 emerging market currencies fell, and the currencies of Egypt and Korea declined the most.

Index performance was mixed as 5 of 11 sectors and 12 of 24 countries rose. The strongest performers included IT, energy, Colombia, Peru, and Brazil, while laggards included communication services, consumer discretionary, Indonesia, India, and the Czech Republic.

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors to and Detractors from Quarter-end Fund Performance

CONTRIBUTORS

  • Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.
  • InPost S.A.
  • SK hynix Inc.
  • B3 S.A.-Brasil, Bolsa, Balcao
  • SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (SKM)

DETRACTORS

  • MakeMyTrip Ltd. (MMYT)
  • HDFC Bank Ltd. (HDB)
  • Tencent Music Entertainment Group (TME)
  • Reliance Industries Ltd.
  • Bharti Airtel Ltd.

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Quarterly Sector Attribution Analysis

Among sectors, IT and financials were the leading contributors during the first quarter, while communication services and consumer discretionary were the largest detractors.

In IT, the fund benefited from strong stock selection. The leading contributors were Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., a Korean semiconductor and consumer electronics company; SK Hynix Inc., a Korean producer of semiconductor and high bandwidth memory chips; and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSM), a leader in outsourced memory chip production based in Taiwan.

The financials sector was a leading contributor due to an underweight allocation to the poorly performing sector. The largest contributors included B3 S.A.-Brasil, Bolsa, Balcao, a Brazilian exchange operator; Samsung Life Insurance Co., Ltd., a Korean insurance provider; and companies the fund did not own.

The communication services sector was the largest detractor due to our overweight exposure to the poorly performing sector. Leading detractors included Tencent Music Entertainment Group, a Chinese online platform for music; Bharti Airtel Ltd., an India-based telecommunications provider with operations in India, South Asia, and Africa; and Sea Ltd. (SE), a Southeast Asian e-commerce and gaming company based in Singapore.

The consumer discretionary sector was a detractor due to stock selection and an overweight exposure to the sector that had lackluster performance. The largest detractors included MakeMyTrip Ltd., an Indian online travel agency; Meituan, a Chinese online platform for local services, including food and grocery delivery; and Trip.com Group Ltd. (TCOM), a Chinese online travel agency.

Quarterly Country Attribution Analysis

Among countries, Korea and Poland were the largest contributors to performance in the quarter while India and China were the largest relative detractors.

Korea was the leading contributor due to stock selection and overweight exposure to the outperforming market. Within the country, the leading contributors included Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.; SK Hynix Inc.; and SK Telecom Co., Ltd., a wireless telecommunications service provider.

Poland was a contributor due to stock selection and an overweight allocation to the well-performing market. The largest stock contributor was InPost S.A., a parcel delivery company.

India was the largest detractor due to stock selection and an overweight exposure to the poorly performing country. The largest detractors in the country included MakeMyTrip Ltd.; HDFC Bank Ltd., a bank and financial services company; and Reliance Industries Ltd., a conglomerate with operations spanning petrochemicals, energy, telecommunications, and retail.

China was a detractor due to stock selection. The largest individual detractors were Tencent Music Entertainment Group; Tencent Holdings Ltd., a gaming and social media platform; and Meituan.

Fund positioning and outlook

The conflict in Iran has introduced significant volatility in global markets. The ultimate outcome, duration, and economic impact remain difficult to assess, and market reactions are being driven by a rapid flow of headlines and commentary. Rather than attempting to anticipate short-term moves, we continue to rely on our fundamental, long-term investment process, using periods of heightened volatility to reassess risk-reward and improve the quality of the portfolio.

Some of the key tailwinds for emerging markets are on hold for now. We entered the year in the near-Goldilocks situation of improving growth and falling inflation. For many countries, this has reversed. However, it is important to note that emerging markets are well poised to weather these challenges. External funding needs are relatively lower, emerging market credit ratings have never been higher, and earnings momentum remains strong. Many of the factors that were pushing inflation down before the crisis were structural in nature and remain in place. Further, in contrast to previous oil crises, emerging markets are much less sensitive to oil prices. The index is increasingly dominated by technology and innovation- led companies, and countries have diversified their reliance on petrochemicals over the past decade. In fact, according to JPMorgan, only 28% of the MSCI EM Index (Net) has a negative beta to oil, and the five-year rolling beta of the overall benchmark to oil prices is at an all-time low. This is not to suggest that emerging markets are immune to the effect of the Iran crisis, but it does suggest some perspective is in order.

The current situation is best viewed as an energy shock with geopolitical uncertainty layered on top. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical artery for global energy flows, and even temporary disruptions can have outsized effects on oil prices, shipping, and market sentiment. Historically, these episodes tend to follow a familiar pattern. Initial price shocks are often followed by a period of stress as higher energy costs feed into inflation and growth concerns. Pressure typically emerges first in smaller, more vulnerable economies, where energy disruptions can spill over into agriculture, food prices, and broader inflation. Governments generally respond through a combination of policy measures, including strategic reserve releases, subsidies, or demand management. Once supply conditions stabilize, inflation pressures tend to ease and risk assets recover. That remains our base case. The primary risks to this outlook are non-linearity (where localized stress spreads more broadly) and the possibility that the conflict proves more prolonged, extending the period of elevated energy prices and uncertainty.

Against this backdrop, the drivers supporting emerging markets remain intact. Valuations remain attractive relative to developed markets, corporate fundamentals continue to improve, and demand for commodities linked to energy transition and AI remains supportive. Ongoing supply chain diversification continues to create opportunities across regions. The last two weeks of data show emerging market fundamentals remain resilient. India's services economy continues to surprise on the upside, ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) consumption rebounded sharply, and inflation across much of the emerging markets in Asia remains contained despite higher energy prices. Periods like this can feel unsettled, but emerging markets are not a trading vehicle. Consistent with our approach outlined above, we view episodes of heightened volatility not as signals to predict near- term market moves but as opportunities to reassess risk- reward, upgrade portfolio quality, and build positions in companies capable of compounding net asset value over time.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector and country returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As a result, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

data.title: Commodities: UAE Exit From OPEC Marks A Big Shift In Oil Market
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy – UAE set to leave OPEC

The big development in oil markets yesterday was news that the UAE is set to leave OPEC from 1 May. This is a significant move and will be a big blow to OPEC. It’s the highest-profile exit from OPEC in recent years.

Prior to the Iran war, the UAE was pumping 3.4m b/d of crude oil (February 2026), making up around 12% of total OPEC output and the third-largest producer within the group. The UAE’s departure will reduce OPEC's effectiveness in managing and influencing the global oil market through supply measures.

The UAE’s exit will increase output, with current production capacity of around 4.85m b/d and plans to reach 5m b/d by 2027. However, before this can be tapped, there must be a resolution in the Persian Gulf that allows for uninhibited energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz once again.

Therefore, in the short term, this development has little impact on the market. But in the medium to longer term, it means more supply for the market. This suggests that the Brent forward curve should move into deeper backwardation.

The UAE has been increasingly frustrated over recent years by its output being constrained by OPEC production quotas, which have kept it well below its potential. In 2024, UAE crude oil production averaged 2.95m b/d- well below capacity.

The timing of the exit was planned well; announcing a departure during a period of significant supply disruption limits the market impact. Had this been announced any other time, we would likely have seen more downward pressure on oil prices.

This would certainly be welcomed by President Trump, as it erodes OPEC’s influence in the oil market, while it should also be beneficial for importers and consumers. The other factor to monitor is whether the UAE’s exit will lead to further fracturing amongst the remaining OPEC members.

However, in the near term, the biggest driver for oil prices remains developments in the Persian Gulf and the timing of a resumption in oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. With no signs of an imminent restart in oil flows, we have revised higher our oil forecasts for the remainder of the year. We now expect ICE Brent to average $104/bbl in 2Q26 and $92/bbl in 4Q26.

Metals - Nickel outperforms on tightening supply signals

Nickel prices climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024 amid intensified supply risks in Indonesia. Lower mining quotas and rising sulphur prices are pressuring laterite-based supply chains, raising concerns over near-term availability.

Higher sulphur costs are weighing on HPAL economics and increasing the risk of downstream curtailments. Indonesia’s reliance on imported sulphur leaves MHP output exposed to cost inflation and potential disruption, while HPAL remains central to global supply growth.

These pressures are now translating into production risk. Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt plans to cut output at its Huafei project in Weda Bay from May, citing higher sulphur prices. The company expects to reduce production by around 50%. The project accounts for roughly 3% of global nickel output.

This supply-driven backdrop continues to support nickel relative to the broader base metals complex, which remains constrained by macro uncertainty. Further cost-driven curtailments or policy-led supply discipline should keep nickel supported, though weaker risk sentiment may limit follow-through.

Copper, meanwhile, remains more sensitive to the macro backdrop. Prices dropped for a fourth day amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty and concerns over global growth.

Although improving physical demand signals in China - including tighter inventories after post‑holiday restocking - are offering some support, overall sentiment remains fragile. Sustained upside in copper is likely to depend on clearer signs of easing geopolitical risks and a more constructive outlook for industrial activity.

Agriculture - Another large Brazilian sugarcane harvest

In its first estimates for the 2026/27 season, CONAB expects Brazil’s sugarcane production to rise 5.3% year-in-year to 709.1mt. If achieved, this would mark the second-largest harvest on record.

The agency said that favourable weather conditions during the crop development phases in 2025 would result in higher yields, rising 3.4% YoY to 77.8t/ha for the 2026/27 season.

In contrast, CONAB expects sugar production to fall 0.5% YoY to 43.9mt in 2026/27. A significant share of sugarcane is being diverted to ethanol production, given that sugar prices have been trading below ethanol parity.

India has allowed a further 2.5mt of wheat exports, according to the Commerce Ministry. This raises total approved shipments to 7.5mt for the current year. With the broader export restrictions remaining in place, shipments are allowed on a selective basis to meet food security requests from overseas buyers.

data.title: Nomura Core Equity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The S&P 500® was volatile in 1Q26, declining 4.33%, as investors grappled with geopolitical shocks and a shifting regulatory landscape. While the market continued to benefit from a robust technology capital investment cycle, escalating tensions in the Middle East and a landmark legal challenge to executive trade authority created significant downward pressure.

The following five factors were the primary market drivers during the quarter:

  • The war in Iran and energy-supply shocks: The escalation of the war in the Middle East triggered a severe energy-supply shock. Brent crude futures surged as hostilities threatened the Strait of Hormuz, creating an inflationary headwind for the broader market while significantly boosting domestic energy valuations.
  • Federal Reserve leadership and global policy signaling: In January 2026, President Trump nominated Kevin Warsh to succeed Jerome Powell as Chair of the Federal Reserve. While the FOMC held the federal funds rate steady in a target range of 3.50% to 3.75%, the leadership transition coincided with a shift in global rhetoric. Major peers, including the European Central Bank (ECB), the Bank of England (BoE), and the Bank of Japan (BoJ), notably adopted more hawkish postures, preparing markets for potential tightening to combat persistent inflation.
  • Supreme Court ruling and tariff pivot: In a landmark decision, the Supreme Court ruled against the Trump administration's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose certain tariffs. In response, the administration moved to replace several of those measures with alternative trade actions, maintaining a policy of "tariff leverage" that contributed to continued market uncertainty.
  • Implementation of the One Big Beautiful Bill: Taking effect in early 2026, this legislation provided a modest fiscal tailwind through corporate and consumer tax stimulus and infrastructure incentives.
  • AI-driven capital equipment cycle: Demand for artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure remained a critical pillar for equities. Massive capital expenditures from hyperscale technology firms, or large-scale data centers, provided a boost to semiconductor and hardware providers, even as many traditional sectors faced downward valuation pressure.

Within the Fund

For 1Q26, Nomura Core Equity Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the S&P 500 Index. Performance was hampered by sector allocation decisions, specifically an underweighting of the energy sector, which surged during the quarter. Conversely, stock selection was a positive contributor, driven by strength in information technology and utilities. Cash was a modestly positive contributor to performance for the quarter, acting as a defensive buffer that mitigated some of the impact from the broader market decline.

Leading contributors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

  • Seagate Technology Holdings PLC (STX) performed well as surging demand for high-capacity storage, driven by AI data center buildouts, led to positive earnings revisions.
  • Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT) shares benefited from positive revisions to semiconductor capital spending plans in response to the demand for AI-related chips.
  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSM) shares continued to appreciate due to its broad leadership in leading-edge manufacturing and exposure to the AI buildout.
  • Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM) shares rose on strong demand for advanced engine components and increased defense spending.
  • BAE Systems PLC shares recovered as global defense budgets increased, driven by the ongoing rearmament cycle in Europe.

Leading detractors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

  • Salesforce Inc. (CRM) shares underperformed as investors grew cautious regarding the pace of enterprise software spending and the monetization timeline for generative AI.
  • Exxon Mobil Corp. (XOM), not owned in the portfolio, was a significant detractor on a relative basis as the stock surged during the energy crisis, acting as a "safe haven" for investors.
  • Airbus SE shares dropped as worsening supply-chain issues and shortages of engine components limited the company's ability to meet delivery forecasts.
  • Accenture PLC (ACN) shares declined as the market reacted to a perceived deceleration in AI services bookings despite solid overall results.
  • SAP SE (SAP) shares fell following a significant miss in cloud-growth expectations, leading to multiple compression as investors questioned the near-term growth narrative.

Outlook

As of the end of 1Q26, bottom-up forecasts now call for 17% earnings growth in 2026, up from 15% at the end of 2025, driven by robust technology sector capital spending, improved industrial earnings, and positive revisions to energy sector profits. We are seeing a notable improvement in key economic indicators, including Institute for Supply Management (ISM) readings on manufacturing, suggesting early improvement in short-cycle industrial activity. The manufacturing ISM had been in contraction territory since November of 2022. Furthermore, continued low employment claims point toward a resilient labor market, supporting sustained growth through the remainder of the year when combined with fiscal stimulus, some of which has been siphoned off by higher energy prices in the short-term.

The current technology investment cycle is increasingly viewed as a structural force for economic expansion. Analysts from Morgan Stanley (MS) estimate that AI-related infrastructure and services will contribute approximately 25% of total US GDP growth in 2026. This “rewiring of growth” suggests that while traditional sectors may fluctuate, the massive deployment of capital into data centers and semiconductor manufacturing has the potential to serve as a powerful engine for both productivity and corporate profit margins.

While we are cautiously optimistic on market performance through the balance of the year given strong expected profit growth, we are closely monitoring our risk levels and portfolio allocations considering several mounting uncertainties. These include the continuation or expansion of the Middle East conflict, potential shifts in the Fed’s policy outlook, and emerging signs of AI-induced labor-market deterioration, which has thus far been largely constrained to high-tech firms that are some of the earliest beneficiaries of AI-driven productivity gains. Additionally, we remain cautious regarding higher inflation and short to mid-term impacts on consumer spending levels driven by elevated energy prices. Ultimately, we believe less predictable geopolitical outcomes will play a greater role than we would like in determining the next major move in the equity market.

Positions Added:

  • Carrier Global Corp. (CARR) is a global leader in high-technology heating, air-conditioning, and refrigeration solutions for residential and commercial markets. HVAC is one of the few industries we cover where demand appears at or near a cycle low. Carrier and the sector should benefit by expansion in the addressable market caused by data-center investment and new, more expensive equipment to conform to environmental regulations.
  • Cboe Global Markets Inc. (CBOE) is a leading financial exchange operator providing platforms for options, futures, equities, and foreign exchange globally. CBOE is not only a beneficiary of heightened financial market volatility in the near term, but we believe it has an interesting pending entry into financial-oriented prediction markets that will likely be additive to growth.
  • Intuit Inc. (INTU) is a global financial technology platform specializing in financial software for individuals and small businesses. Its products include TurboTax and QuickBooks. We believe Intuit is one of the least likely software vendors to be displaced or affected by AI adoption given its mission critical positions in small business accounting and consumer tax preparation.
  • Parker-Hannifin Corp. (PH) is a manufacturer of motion and control technologies, providing precision-engineered solutions for diverse industrial and aerospace markets. While the company has grown through a generally suboptimal backdrop for global industrial activity, we believe growth can accelerate as short-cycle activity improves.
  • PNC Financial Services Group Inc. (PNC) is one of the largest diversified financial services institutions in the US, offering retail and commercial banking and asset management. We are less sanguine about further improvement in consumer credit (the reason for our sale of Capital One Financial) but anticipate a structural pickup in commercial and industrial lending that should benefit PNC’s future growth profile.

Positions Eliminated:

  • Live Nation Entertainment Inc. (LYV)
  • The Progressive Corp. (PGR)

data.title: Supply Shocks Plus Inflation Bind The Fed's Hands
data.description:
By Matthew Schaffer

Energy-driven inflation and slowing growth have put the Fed in a difficult position. A look at monetary policy rules shows why holding rates steady may be the most prudent response.

The Federal Open Market Committee is widely expected to leave its policy rate unchanged at this week’s meeting. The CME Group puts the odds that the FOMC will continue to target the federal funds rate within the 3.5 to 3.75 percent range at 99.5 percent. But the near certainty regarding this week’s decisions masks the growing problem Fed officials face.

The rise in energy prices tied to the conflict with Iran is the sort of negative supply shock that makes monetary policy especially difficult. It puts upward pressure on inflation even as it threatens to slow growth and weaken employment.

That puts the Federal Reserve in an awkward position. Under its dual mandate, the Fed is supposed to promote both price stability and maximum employment. Ordinarily, Fed officials have the luxury of focusing on one of those objectives at a time.

When inflation is increasing, the Fed can raise rates to cool demand. When growth slows and unemployment rises, it can cut rates to support spending and hiring. An adverse supply shock is different because it simultaneously threatens both goals.

What the Rules Say

The difficulty posed by adverse supply shocks makes it all the more important to seek guidance from monetary rules. The latest Monetary Rules Report from AIER’s Sound Money Project shows that the Fed’s current policy rate already sits near the lower end of the recommended range.

The Taylor Rule remains the most familiar place to start. It says that the Fed should set interest rates higher when inflation runs above target and lower when economic activity or employment falls below sustainable levels.

Using the most recent data available, the original version of the rule points to a federal funds rate of 4.66 percent. A modified version that minimizes interest rate volatility and accounts for forecasts of future inflation implies a policy rate of 3.99 percent. If anything, the Taylor Rule suggests Fed officials ought to consider an increase in the federal funds rate target.

Rules based on nominal gross domestic product, or NGDP, suggest somewhat lower rates, with an NGDP level rule at 3.93 percent and an NGDP growth rule at 3.53 percent. These estimates are in line with the current stance of policy and support the expected decision to hold steady at 3.5–3.75 percent.

How Rules Account for Supply Shocks

In normal circumstances, both types of rules provide a useful way to translate incoming data into a policy rate prescription. But supply shocks make the Taylor Rule harder to interpret, because they create conflicting signals.

Higher energy prices put upward pressure on inflation, which points toward tighter policy. At the same time, they raise production costs and squeeze household budgets, which can weaken output and employment, pointing toward easier policy. As a result, the Taylor Rule gets pulled in opposite directions.

That tension has also shown up in recent commentary from policymakers. Some of the more dovish voices inside the Fed and around the administration - who have been quite eager to lower rates - have admitted that any cuts are more likely to come later in the year, after the current Middle East conflict subsides. That shift reflects how difficult it is to formulate policy when a negative supply shock strains both sides of the Fed’s mandate at once.

A Better Guide During Supply Shocks

This is where rules based on nominal spending become especially useful. NGDP is simply the total dollar value of spending in the economy. Its growth rate combines inflation and real output growth into a single measure.

That makes it an especially useful guide when supply shocks hit. Instead of forcing policymakers to weigh inflation and growth separately, NGDP rules ask a broader question: what is happening to total spending?

The NGDP growth rule, for instance, suggests that monetary policymakers aim for 4 percent annual growth in nominal spending. The 4 percent benchmark reflects the fact that the Fed targets a 2 percent inflation rate and annual output growth tends to average 2 percent.

It effectively wraps both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate into a single statistic. Importantly, in the context of a negative supply shock, it also accommodates offsetting movements in those two objectives.

For instance, if spiking energy prices push inflation to 3 percent and pull real growth down to 1 percent, overall NGDP growth would remain at 4 percent, and the Fed would be justified in keeping rates steady - despite inflation moving temporarily above target.

In other words, if oil prices rise because of geopolitical conflict, inflation may move higher even though overall spending is not accelerating in a way that calls for tighter monetary policy.

At the same time, weaker real growth alone does not necessarily mean the Fed should cut, so long as nominal spending remains reasonably stable. Looking at NGDP helps policymakers avoid overreacting to only one dimension of the shock.

In the April report, the NGDP rules are broadly consistent with leaving policy unchanged. The NGDP growth rule, in particular, suggests that current policy is roughly on target, with the most recent data showing NGDP growth of 4.2 percent - very close to the rule’s 4 percent benchmark.

That figure is backward-looking, so it is reasonable to worry that it may not fully capture recent developments tied to the conflict in the Middle East. Even so, more recent inflation data and forecasts of real output growth still point to nominal spending growth of around 4 percent. That reinforces the case for keeping policy where it is.

What This Means for the Fed

Supply shocks create some of the most challenging problems for monetary policymakers because they blur the line between inflation risk and economic weakness. That is what makes the current moment so uncomfortable for Fed officials. But discomfort need not mean confusion.

The leading rules still offer a useful signal: when overall nominal spending remains close to trend, policymakers should be careful not to overreact to either dimension of a supply disturbance. Fed officials can remain confident by keeping policy within the range offered by the leading monetary policy rules.
