data.title: Nexxen International: Cheap For A Reason Until Programmatic Growth Reaccelerates
data.description:
Investment Summary

Nexxen (NEXN) is an AdTech company that integrates a demand-side platform (DSP), a supply-side platform (SSP) and a data platform. The business seems to be moving from weaker non-programmatic business lines to a more strategic programmatic mix, including programmatic video, Connected TV (CTV) and advanced TV advertising.

The market is looking skeptically at the fact that revenue was more or less flat in FY25, with CTV revenue declining and adjusted EBITDA barely growing. At the same time, the stock is very cheap on an EV/EBITDA basis, and the company has repurchased a large portion of its share count in the last few years, which should provide some support to the business valuation.

The question is whether Nexxen's programmatic core can grow fast enough to offset the decline of non-core, non-programmatic revenue and eventually generate significant total revenue growth. Until we see this happening, the FY26 contribution ex-TAC guide management provided seems encouraging but not sufficient to make it a compelling buy. We believe Nexxen is a hold/watchlist as of today.

Business Overview

Nexxen is a part of the programmatic advertising ecosystem as it operates both the DSP (used by advertisers to buy inventory) and the SSP side (used by publishers and media owners to sell inventory) and adds a data layer on top of it for its targeting, measurement, and optimisation.

The programmatic side of the business (the automated, data-driven buying and selling of targeted ad inventory) increasingly became Nexxen's core revenue base. By contrast, non-programmatic revenue declined significantly, making Nexxen an asset-light, profitable, cash-generative business.

The concern remains that AdTech is a competitive business, cyclical and hard to differentiate within. The key to unlocking that situation appears to be CTV and data assets, which could become genuinely important to large buyers like The Trade Desk (TTD). As a leading DSP, TTD benefits from broader access to differentiated CTV inventory and data signals, which helps explain why the partnership with Nexxen matters.

What The Market Thinks

The market likely sees Nexxen as a cheap AdTech name that deserves its discount, as the company has not demonstrated revenue compounding so far.

NEXN went through several strategic evolutions and integrations over the years, yet the headline growth is far from impressive. The bearish arguments are pretty simple to grasp: Nexxen has not yet shown convincing headline growth despite the strategic shift towards programmatic and CTV.

The market is likely questioning whether NEXN is a growing programmatic platform or a legacy AdTech business transitioning away from weaker business lines.

What The Numbers Actually Show

The numbers are showing a cleaner mix but not yet a clean growth story.

In FY25, total revenue was $364.8M against $365.5M in FY24, essentially flat. Programmatic revenue came at $340.6M in FY25 (roughly 93% of total revenue), up from 89% in FY24. CTV was a major component of programmatic revenue, representing 32% of that segment (down from 35% in FY24). Non-programmatic came down dramatically YoY from $41M in FY24 to $24.2M in FY25. This means the core business grew but not enough to produce headline revenue growth.

Contribution ex-TAC (excluding the performance media costs tied to non-core activities) did increase by 3% in FY25 to $353.1M, showing that the business mix is improving. That said, it is more a modest improvement than a clear growth inflection, especially given flat total revenue and the recent weak CTV trend.

There could be an acceleration of programmatic revenue in FY26, as guided by management. During the Q4/25 earnings call, they also shared that Q1-to-date contribution ex-TAC and programmatic revenue were trending ahead of initial expectations following the strongest January and February in the company's history. That said, we would prefer to see Q1/26's numbers before treating the re-acceleration as proven, especially given the weak Q4/25 results and the company's long-term revenue trajectory, range-bound between roughly $330M and $365M annual revenue since 2021.

The profitability is also weaker than the adjusted EBITDA headline suggests, although it's more nuanced than a simple margin collapse. In FY25, adjusted EBITDA was essentially flat (rising 1% to $115.1M), but the Q4/25 figure declined 23% YoY to $33.9M. GAAP profitability declined even more, with operating profit declining 21% for the year (despite gross profit being roughly stable), with a 47% decline in Q4/25. Net income fell 29% for the year and 58% in Q4/25. Non-IFRS net income also declined 8% in FY25 and 41% in Q4/25. This shows that the pressure is not just GAAP accounting noise.

Revenue and gross profit stayed more or less flat. Key cost lines like R&D, sales & marketing, depreciation & amortisation and SBC, increased significantly, which suggests that Nexxen is investing in the future of its programmatic and data business lines. Looking at Q4/25 alone, the related revenue acceleration has not yet clearly shown up, although management's Q1-to-date comments suggest that the trajectory may improve in 2026. Overall, an improving programmatic mix should translate to better revenue quality and profitability, so the next few quarters need to see clearer evidence of such improvements. So far, the recent weak CTV trend and pressure on GAAP earnings, despite the record contribution ex-TAC, remain concerning.

Nexxen generated $110.1M of operating cash flow in FY25. The business ended the year with $133.3M of cash and no long-term debt. It used that cash flow to repurchase roughly $101.7M of stock last year, with 38.5% of shares outstanding being bought back since March 2022. Recently, they announced another $40M share buyback program. This is by far the strongest offset to the flat growth and weaker profitability trend.

FY26 guidance looks decent on paper, with contribution ex-TAC expected to grow roughly 8% YoY, programmatic revenue and adjusted EBITDA expected to grow roughly 10%. Management also expects growth in CTV, self-service, and data products. In other words, FY25 can still be seen as an investment year, but only if the next quarters begin to confirm visible programmatic acceleration, CTV recovery, and adjusted EBITDA growth.

The Decisive Variable

Can Nexxen's programmatic core grow enough to offset the non-programmatic decline to create visible revenue growth? This is the key variable, as the current valuation (more below) is only compelling if EBITDA and cash flow are sustainable. If programmatic grows only 5% (like it did in FY25) while non-core business lines continue to shrink, the stock may remain cheap but not necessarily exciting.

Management's FY26 guidance implies roughly 8% contribution ex-TAC growth and about 10% programmatic revenue growth. This would be an improvement if confirmed in the coming financials. Q1/26 is going to be the real first test to see whether FY26 guidance indeed reflects a real growth inflection; otherwise, headline optics will remain less appealing as total revenue may remain pressured if the non-core revenue continues to decline.

Advanced TV, CTV, mobile in-app, and smart TV home-screen advertising likely need to become key contributors for management's guidance to be viewed as sustainable. That is why the CTV rebound matters so much, as it seems to be the segment most directly tied to the bull case, yet it was weak in FY25.

The partnerships with V/VIDAA and TTD are meaningful because they suggest Nexxen could provide a smart TV supply and data layer that large DSPs want to access. The encouraging thing is that management specifically said that the company's strong traction in early 2026 stems directly from the 2025 infrastructure investments and expanded DSP partnerships, which is what bullish investors want to see.

AI is another factor to watch. If AI-driven search ends up reducing traditional browsing activity, then it could put pressure on the advertising ecosystem of the open web. Nexxen's response is to shift more resources toward channels less exposed to that disruption (CTV, mobile in-app, and smart TV home-screen advertising) while it integrates nexAI into its DSP and data platform to improve planning, optimisation and workflow efficiency. This is positive, but we are still waiting to see this translate into higher revenue growth and margin leverage.

Valuation

Nexxen screens are cheaper than its peers based on current valuation data:

Company Role EV/Sales EV/EBITDA Revenue Growth YoY Comment
Nexxen Integrated DSP/SSP, CTV/data ~0.8x ~4.6x -0.2% Cheap, but growth unproven
The Trade Desk Premium independent DSP ~3.5x ~15.0x 18.5% Higher-quality, larger-scale demand platform
Magnite (MGNI) SSP/CTV supply platform ~2.7x ~14x 6.8% More direct CTV/SSP comp
Viant (DSP) DSP/CTV-focused platform ~0.6x ~7.3x 19% Closer small-cap DSP comp

The discount to peers is not irrational, as Nexxen is clearly showing the weakest growth YoY relative to its competitors that are growing close to 20% YoY in the case of Viant and The Trade Desk.

Given this gap, a rerating would require Nexxen to show a clearer path toward its 10% programmatic revenue growth target, CTV returning to growth with visible total revenue expansion and adjusted EBITDA growth in line with guidance, while the share count continues to shrink aggressively. CTV needs to recover with clear monetisation of smart TV home-screen inventory. Data needs to show that its access to V/VIDAA's Automatic Content Recognition data can translate into visible data revenue or higher media spend.

As of today, Nexxen remains a show-me story, as those elements are not yet visible enough in the reported numbers.

Conclusion

Nexxen is a cheap, cash-generative AdTech platform. The story is supported by very aggressive share repurchases. The programmatic mix is improving, which should help the business move forward. The share count is being reduced aggressively, the business has a strong net cash position, and management is guiding for decent programmatic growth in FY26.

The growth case is still not proven, though, as revenue was flat in FY25 due to non-core revenue shrinking roughly as fast as core revenue grew. Profitability was hurt, especially in the latter part of the year, which warrants caution. The FY26 guidance is fairly encouraging, and management's Q1-to-date comments suggest early momentum.

That said, it should not be seen as decisive before it gets supported by revenue growth in early 2026, especially in the programmatic business line. Based on the information available today, the stock looks compelling enough to be put on a watchlist but not enough for a Buy.
